title: Shree. Basavarajayya S/O. Suvvi ... vs Smt. Suvvi Sarvamangalamma W/O.Late ... on 21 February, 2013
body: Shree. Basavarajayya S/O. Suvvi ... vs Smt. Suvvi Sarvamangalamma W/O.Late ... on 21 February, 2013 Author: B.V.Nagarathna Bench: B.V.Nagarathna 1 IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA CIRCUIT BENCH AT DHARWAD DATED THIS THE 21ST DAY OF FEBRUARY, 2013 BEFORE THE HON'BLE MRS.JUSTICE B.V.NAGARATHNA M.S.A.599/2012 BETWEEN: Shree. S. BASAVARAJAYYA, S/O. SUVVI PORAYYA , AGE: 74 YEARS, OCC: AGRICULTURE. R/O. VADDU VILLAGE, TQ: SANDUR. DIST: BELLARY-583 103. ... APPELLANT (By Sri. SHIVARAJ HIREMATH, ADV. ) AND 1. SMT.SUVVI SARVAMANGALAMMA, W/O.LATE SUVVI PAMPAPATHEYYA, AGE: 86 YEARS, OCC: AGRICULTURIST. R/O. 606, 7TH MAIN, 3RD STAGE, 3RD BLOCK, BASAVESHWAR NAGAR, BANGALORE-560 079. 2. S. CHANDRAKANTA @ CHANDRAKANTA VADDU, S/O.LATE SUVVI PAMPAPATHEYYA, AGE: 49 YEARS, OCC: AGRICULTURE. R/O. 606, 7TH MAIN, 3RD STAGE, 3RD BLOCK, BASAVESHWAR NAGAR, BANGALORE-560 079. ... RESPONDENTS (By R1 & R2 are SD and un-reptd.) 2 THIS MSA IS FILED U/O.43 RULE 1(u) OF THE CPC , AGAINST THE JUDGEMENT AND DECREE DTD:10.07.2012 PASSED IN R.A.NO.137/2009 ON THE FILE OF THE PRESIDING OFFICER, FAST TRACK COURT-III, AT HOSPET, ALLOWING THE APPEAL FILED AGAINST THE JUDGEMENT AND DECREE DATED:15.07.2009 PASSED IN O.S. NO.19/2008 ON THE FILE OF THE CIVIL JUDGE (SR.DVN.) AT KUDLIGI, DISMISSING THE SUIT FILED FOR DECLARATION, AND ETC. THIS APPEAL COMING ON FOR ADMISSION, THIS DAY, THE COURT DELIVERED THE FOLLOWING: JUDGMENT Though this Miscellaneous Second Appeal is posted for admission, I have nevertheless heard the learned Counsel for the appellant on merits. The respondents are served and are unrepresented. 2. The appellant is the defendant in O. S.19 /2008. The respondent-plaintiffs filed the said suit seeking a declaration that they are the absolute owners of the suit schedule property and for possession and mesne profits. According to the plaintiffs, one Poraiah was the propositus. He had three wives. The husband of the first plaintiff Pampapathaiah was the son of first wife. The defendant is the son of second wife. After the death of Poraiah, the 3 husband of the first plaintiff being the eldest son became the Kartha of the joint family. He died suddenly on 16.5.1969 at Karjigi village in Hassan District. Thereafter, the defendant became the Manager of the joint family consisting of the plaintiffs, defendant and other members. On account of disharmony in the family, oral partition took place in the year 1975 and the same was reduced into writing in the year 1978. Thereafter, the plaintiff No.2 became the manager of the family. Since Plaintiff No.1 was an illiterate woman, the defendant being the eldest person became defacto guardian of the plaintiff No.1 and started managing the properties that fell to the share of plaintiff No.1 also. 3. The land bearing Sy.No.262 was purchased by the husband of the 1st plaintiff on 6.3.1950 and the Khatha was standing in his name till his death in 1969. After his death, the names of the plaintiffs were mutated in respect of the said land till the year 1982-83. But the defendant has claimed that the said land has fallen to his 4 share and he had illegally mutated his name in the revenue records. The plaintiffs pleaded that they had reposed confidence in the defendant as the Manager of the family. The defendant has taken undue advantage of this confidence reposed in him by the plaintiffs and has got his name entered in the revenue records. That, three months prior to the filing of the suit plaintiffs came to know about the illegal entries. That the plaintiffs are in possession of the other properties, but the defendant is trying to dispossess them on the strength of the illegal entry in the revenue records. Hence, plaintiffs filed suit for declaration of title and for possession of their suit lands. 4. On receipt of suit summons and notice from the trial Court, the defendant appeared and filed his written statement and additional written statement admitting the relationship between the parties. He has stated that an oral partition took place in the year 1975 and the same was reduced into writing on 3.11.1978. 5 That the entire property of the joint family was divided into five shares, out of which one share was given to the plaintiffs in respect of the land bearing Sy.Nos.283, 284-A, 292, measuring 1.82 acres, 0.26 acres and 0.75 acres respectively, and the house of 3 ankanas and cash of Rs.29,000/-. That defendant got the suit schedule land to his share along with other landed properties. That, the parties are enjoying respective shares of the property since the year 1975. That the suit of the plaintiffs is barred by limitation. The defendant has leased a part of the suit property to the manufacturer of bricks, for Dhaba hotel and for Airtel Tower and Vodafone Tower since the last five years. That, he is enjoying the suit schedule property since the date of partition to the knowledge of the plaintiffs. That, the suit land was purchased by the husband of the plaintiff out of the income of the joint family. Defendant denied all other averments of the suit and sought for dismissal of the suit. 6 5. On the basis of the rival pleadings, the Trial Court framed the following issues and additional issues for its consideration: "i) Whether the plaintiffs prove that they are the owners of the suit schedule land? ii) Whether the plaintiffs prove that the defendant has dispossessed them from the suit schedule land as averred in para-10 of the plaint? iii) Whether the defendant proves that the suit is barred by Limitation? iv) Whether the plaintiffs are entitled for the reliefs sought for? v) What order or decree? Addl.issue No.1) Whether the defendant proves that the suit is bad for non-joinder of necessary parties?" 6. In support of their case, the plaintiffs examined two witnesses and produced 14 documents marked as Ex.P.1 to Ex.P.14, while the defendant examined himself as DW.1 and produced 34 documents which are marked as Ex.D.1 to D.34. 7 7. On the basis of the said evidence, the Trial Court answered Issue Nos.1, 2, 4 and Addl. Issue No.1 in the negative and answered issue No.3 in the affirmative and dismissed the suit of the plaintiffs. 8. Being aggrieved by the dismissal of the suit the plaintiffs filed R.A.No.137/2009 before the I Appellate Court. On hearing the learned Counsel for the parties, the I Appellate Court framed the following points for its consideration: "1) Is the Trial Court not justified in holding that the suit is barred by Limitation? 2) Is the Trial Court not justified in holding that plaintiffs are not the absolute owners of the Suit schedule property? 3) Is it just and necessary to set aside the Judgment and decree and remand the matter for fresh disposal to the Trial Court? 4) What Decree or Order?" It answered Point Nos.1 and 3 in the affirmative and held that point No.2 would not survive for consideration and allowed the appeal by setting aside the judgment and 8 decree of the Trial Court and remanded the matter back to the Trial Court with a direction to club the suit with O.S.No.12/2010 for a common trial and dispose of the suits by a common judgment. The said order of remand is assailed by the defendant in this Miscellaneous Second Appeal. 9. I have heard the learned Counsel for the appellant. 10. It is firstly contended, that the I Appellate Court was not right in holding that the Trial Court was not justified in coming to a conclusion that the suit is barred by limitation. Elaborating the said contention it is stated that there was partition between the family members way back in the year 1975 which was reduced into writing in the year 1978. The plaintiffs and defendant were allotted their respective shares in the suit properties belonging to the family to an extent of 1/5th share. That the suit schedule property was allotted to the defendant. That the plaintiffs have no right, title or interest in the said suit. 9 Since 1975 the defendant has been enjoying the suit property and his name has been entered in the mutation records. Therefore, the suit filed by the plaintiffs seeking declaration of title and possession is barred by time. He also stated that after the disposal of O.S.No.19/2008 the daughter of the 1st plaintiff filed a suit in O. S.12 /2010 seeking partition and separation possession in the properties of the joint family. The said suit is pending trial before the trial Court. That the application filed by the daughter of 1st plaintiff for impleadment in O. S.19 /2008 before the I Appellate Court was dismissed by order dated 7.6.2011 on the premise that she is not a necessary party for the suit. Hence, it is contended that after dismissing the said application for impleadment the I Appellate Court was not right in remanding the matter back to the Trial Court to try the same along with the partition suit O. S.12 /2010 filed by the impleading applicant before the Trial Court, by letting in fresh common evidence. He submitted that the approach of the I Appellate Court was not right since the cause of action in O. S.19 /08 is quite 10 distinct from the cause of action in O. S.12 /2010. The reliefs claimed by the respective plaintiffs are also distinct and hence I Appellate Court could not have remanded the matter to the Trial Court for retrial along with O. S.12 /2010. 11. As already stated the respondents are served but are not represented. 12. Having heard the learned Counsel for the appellant and on perusal of the material on record, the only point that arises for my consideration is: "Whether the I Appellate Court was right in remanding the matter to the Trial Court to club the same with O. S.12 /2010 for fresh common trial?" 13. From the material on record, the relationship between the parties is not in dispute. The suit filed by the plaintiffs is one for declaration of title and for the consequential relief of possession for mesne profits. It is 11 the case of the plaintiffs that the suit schedule property is not joint family property. It is the self-acquired property of the husband of the 1st plaintiff and father of the 2nd plaintiff, viz., Pampapathaiah- the son of the original propositus Poraiah. The plaintiffs have not accepted any partition in the joint family nor have they accepted that the oral partition was reduced into writing in the year 1978. On the other hand, it is the defence of the defendant that on account of the oral partition followed by the partition in writing dated 3.11.1978, the suit schedule property has fallen to the share of the defendant and that he has been in enjoyment of the suit property along with other properties and it is pursuant to the said partition that his name has been entered in the mutation records. It is in this context that the plea of limitation has been raised by the defendant to contend that since he has common ownership and possession of the landed property since the year 1978 the suit filed in the year 2008 was not maintainable. In order to establish this fact, no 12 corroborative evidence has been let in with regard to the oral partition and neither is there any document showing oral partition having been reduced into writing on 3.11.1978. In the absence of such a document, the plea of limitation would fall to the ground. 14. That apart, that on a perusal of the copy of the plaint which has been made available by the learned Counsel for the appellant during the course of the arguments, it is noted that the plaintiffs have pleaded that the cause of action has arisen in the last week of April 2008 when the defendant tried to illegally dispossess the plaintiffs from the suit land. Therefore, the suit is filed in the July 2008. Having regard to the fact that the memorandum of partition dated 3.11.1978 has not been marked in the evidence and that plea of said partition has not been accepted by the plaintiffs, the I appellate Court rightly held that the plea of limitation is not available to the defendant and that the Trial Court was not right in dismissing the suit on maintainability, by answering Issue 13 No.3 in the affirmative. Therefore, that portion of the order of the I Appellate Court would not call for any interference. However, the I Appellate Court thereafter has not proceeded to consider as to whether the findings given by the Trial Court on all the issues raised by it is just and proper. In fact, after dismissing the application for impleadment sought by the daughter of the 1st plaintiff who is also the plaintiff in O. S.12 /2010, the I Appellate Court was not right in simply setting aside the judgment and decree of the Trial Court on merits without re- appreciating the evidence on record and directing that there be fresh common trial with O.S.No.12/2010 which is the suit filed by the daughter of the plaintiff No.1. 15. As already stated the reliefs claimed by the plaintiffs are on a particular cause of action and the same is distinct from the cause of action that the daughter of the plaintiff No.1 has in seeking the relief of partition. No doubt the suit schedule property may also be one of the 14 items in O. S.12 /2010. But that is no reason for the plaintiffs' suit to be clubbed along with O. S.12 /2010. The said O. S.12 /2010 is one for partition and separate possession claimed by the daughter of the 1st plaintiff, which is to be considered on its own merits. In this suit the question is as to whether there was a previous partition between all the joint family properties and whether the said partition has been proved. If there was no partition in the joint family properties, then the question is whether this schedule property is the joint family property of the deceased or the separate property of the deceased Pampapathaiah. This aspect had to be considered by the I Appellate Court since the Trial Court had not only answered the preliminary issue with regard to the maintainability of the suit on the question of limitation but also on all other issues touching upon the merits of the case. 16. Therefore, the order of remand made by the I appellate Court is not in accordance with law. The said 15 order is set aside. The matter is now remanded back to the I Appellate Court to re-appreciate the evidence on record dehors the issue of limitation, in the light of the reliefs sought by the plaintiffs, and then to come to an independent conclusion as to whether the judgment and decree of the Trial Court is correct or not. 17. In the result, the appeal is allowed in-part. The matter is remanded back to the I Appellate Court to dispose of the Regular Appeal in accordance with law in the light of the observations made above. Sd/- JUDGE *sub/
